videos.title: Dave Ramsey Explains The Housing Market In 2025
videos.text: [Music] Brought to you by the Every Dollar app. Start budgeting for free today. >> Hey Dave, can I ask you a quick real estate question real quick? >> You can. >> All right. So, this might be bad math in my head. So, let's say we're in Nashville, Tennessee, where the real estate has gone way up in value over the last 5 10 years. Right. >> Okay. >> So, let's say the house I bought back in 2020 is quote unquote worth double. >> Mhm. >> And I put on the market for double what I paid for it in 2020. >> Mhm. And let's say because of the influx in inventory, I have to drop the price a bit. >> And somebody comes in and bids $10,000 left. So it's just a little under double. >> I feel like on social media and whatever it the report is house prices are falling. But the way I look at it is I'm still on house money because it's worth almost double what I paid for it. And just because I take is that is that a bad way for me to look at the real estate investment? Okay. Um, do you get what I'm saying? >> No, you're it is house money, but it's it's different than that. The reason social media is wrong is for a different reason. Market value of a property by definition. When I went to real estate, when I got my four-year degree in real estate, I have a degree in real estate and finance. If you go to appraisal class and you go to take even take your real estate test, they teach you this. What a market the definition of market value of a piece of real estate is what a willing buyer is able to give a willing seller and and willing uh when neither are under duress. Okay? So in other words, if you're getting foreclosed on, that's not a valid say you can't use that sale when you're doing an appraisal, >> right? >> Because it it does not establish market value because one of the parties is under duress. Okay. If you've had a house on the if you bought another house and you're having to sell your house out of desperation because you shouldn't have bought the other house and now you got two house payments. One of the parties is under duress. >> So they might sell it quote unquote below market value. >> Yes. >> Just to get rid of it. >> Yes. Because but it has but it does not establish market value. So when someone says house prices are going down, they mean market values are going down. Market values have not gone down. But there are some parties because the market has been sluggish that are under duress. >> Gotcha. >> And are sellers. They may have leverage what you call a motivated seller for one reason or another. >> And uh house has been sitting on the market and they lower the price uh below what the appraisal would be uh what a willing buyer, but they're no longer without duress. They have duress meaning there's a stress. They're in a stress situation. >> Okay? >> And so that doesn't establish market value. that doesn't establish what home prices are doing. Now, but if you're in a uh a a sellers market, then the buyers are under duress because they can't that's when you get 83 offers on the house. If you're in a buyer market, which we haven't seen in a long time because inventories have never kept up with demand. But if you're in a buyer market, that means there's houses everywhere and the buyers could come in and cherrypick what they want and they can demand stuff from sellers because the sellers are under more stress and that drives prices down. But we haven't seen that in two decades. >> But even if if market value went down 20%. I'm still up. >> You're still on house. But but house prices would have gone down if that was the case. >> Correct. There you go. So, social media would be correct. But social media is a drama queen. >> Correct. >> It's not functioning on anything except, you know, some some 20some year old living in his mother's basement having a little snowflake attack. >> And that's your social media. That's not anything to do with the actual reality of what's happening in the market. That's just somebody pissed off because they feel like they got boxed out of the market because they're a barista after getting a PhD. >> Well, they're pissed off because they did some napkin math and looked at the wrong internet website and said, "My house is worth $1.2 $2 million. They get an offer for $700 and in their soul they feel like they lost $500,000 when they >> if it was not if it was not worth 1.2. >> That's exactly right. >> Yeah. It was never worth that. And so is there actual market value? So if you're going to do an appraisal on a piece of residential real estate, you find three comparable sales within the last 90 days, comparable in area, comparable in attributes, and comparable in square footage. and you adjust for square footage. If one's a five bedroomedroom, one's a fourbedroom, one's got six baths, one's got four baths, one's got a fivecar garage, one's got a three-car garage, you adjust for the differences, and you you do that and then you take the average of those three after adjustments and you have a residential appraisal. But the the qualification is all three of those comparable sales cannot have had a buyer or a seller under duress. So, you can't use two foreclosures in the neighborhood. Gotcha. Okay. as your comp, as your comparable sale. Otherwise, you have an invalid appraisal. >> What they do in 2008 when whole neighborhoods were getting wiped out. >> Well, then you've got a new market. The market established that this neighborhood is foreclosure neighborhood. >> So, it's it drove it down. >> Yep. >> Because everything in there was. So, if you had like those town houses and stuff where they, you know, where these bogus investment deals, that's what all happened in 2008. And the mark the uh mortgage bank securities, all that crap crashed. And so, they started punting on these loans left and right. And so they end up with whole neighborhoods back. Now you got a complete reset on that neighborhood. But that's not a statement of real estate. That's a statement of that neighborhood. >> That neighborhood was full of investment real estate. 100% renters and uh 100% of the investors in air quotes were walked away, >> And so now you got to reset. >> Um and now we've got, okay, what will people pay in that neighborhood that where neither are under duress? And that takes about a generation to get through that. Not a generation of people, but a generation of sales because the bank when they take it back, they're under >> Ah, okay. >> So, when the bank resells it, you can't count that. That's a a real estate owned, an REO. You can't count that as your appraisal. And so, that's what we're getting into. So, no, we that's what this thing I just said. Go to our website. We track this stuff. There's 1,36,1 homes on the market right now. We know exactly I'm here on the market. We know exactly when we're tracking all this stuff in detail and you can go there and find the actual data and year month over month over month every month this year median house price which is the middle not the average it's the middle of house prices is what a median is in statistics goes has gone up every single month. It's not gone up much it's gone up like a thousand bucks >> or 2,000 >> but it's not crashing like every >> it's not going down is the point. It's going up. >> Yeah. and there's good inventory and there's good demand and everybody's sitting around waiting to see if the Fed chairman's really going to get fired and if we're really going to see some interest rates adjusted >> and uh once they get past that waiting game probably about September you may see this market take off like a dad gum >> hair on fire thing. September could be wild >> in in terms of house prices going up again. But we're not We've been telling you guys this out there and it's proven to be true. I've been telling you this for five years. House prices are not going down. This is not a bubble. A bubble is when there is an is when the prices have gone up faster than the demand. Demand has outpaced inventory. Demand is higher than supply. Every time you see that in economics, you see prices go up. It's a simple thing. It's seventh grade economics if anybody taught economics in seventh grade anymore. But that's it. I mean, when there's a shortage of goods or services, the price goes up on those. When there's an overabundance of goods or services, the price goes down on those. It's very simple. And you really can't hardly figure out anytime in economics with an open market anyway, that that gets violated. Um, it just shows up that way every single time given a given a half a minute. But, uh, I mean, you get weird anomalies like COVID and that kind of stuff that hit hit a marketplace. It takes a little while to get the wrinkle out of that out of the sheet on that. Like the supply chain stuff, same thing. But the uh this is it. It's just it's the problem is everybody's just so frustrated that wants a house and can't get one right now. >> Sure. >> And so they're so they're throwing all these darts out there that they call truth to try to make themselves feel better about it. And it's just not it doesn't change anything. You still have to do the math. Create your free every dollar budget today. The simplest way to budget for your

videos.title: Real estate 2026 outlook: Why the housing market could loosen up next year
videos.text: So, let's get right into this report, Joel. So, mortgage rates, you say here, expected to stay above 6% in 2026. Your forecast, Joel, if I have this right, is 6.3% average mortgage rates next year. Let's start there, Joel. Why Why do they stay above 6%, Joel? And what does that mean for affordability next year? really we see 2026 as a year of steadying across a lot of dimensions of the housing market. Mortgage rates are one piece of that. Uh we expect uh the the Federal Reserve's cuts in December to already be priced into the market and we expect stability going forward with how monetary policy is handled and how the housing market responds. And so I think this mortgage rate stability is really welcome news for folks who have been on the sidelines for the last couple years unsure of whether they can get into the market or not because of how volatile rates have been. So we expect 6.3% average throughout 2026 and ending the year at 6.3%. >> And affordability it improves Joel you think? So I have this right because you think incomes will rise faster than prices. >> That's right. We project prices of homes to rise at about 2.2 2% over 2026 uh which we think is going to be a little bit below inflation and a little bit below income. So real and adjusted price of homes uh will actually improve a little bit in 2026 and the improvements in mortgage rates from this year's average which is about 6.6% play a part in there as well. >> Home sales you point out here Joel they hit a 30-year low in 2024. You think they improved slightly this year per your forecast. What happens in 2026 Joel? 2026, we're projecting a 1.7% increase in home sales. Uh getting up into the 4.1 million range. Uh still pretty subdued from what a couple years ago were. Uh but not down to the 30-year lows that we've been seeing uh in 2024 and 2025. So really, it's a little bit more of the same, just slightly steadying and a modest improvement in home sales volume. >> What about inventory, Joel? I'm curious what you forecast next year. Are buyers going to have more options? Buyers will have more options across the country. We project an 8.9% increase in the number of homes for sale. And this, of course, isn't uniform across the country. In different pockets, in different markets, we'll see inventory rising and falling at different rates. But overall, more options for buyers to choose from. Uh less competition, so uh more negotiating power for buyers. >> You point out, Jo, I mean, there are regional differences. Are there certain regions of the country, Joel, you all expect to to outperform next year? A lot of our strongest markets are in the Northeast and Midwest uh in terms of projections for 2026. You know, these are places where we expect sales volume to grow and prices to grow uh because inventory is more constrained. While in the South and West, inventory recovery has been a lot stronger. There's more new construction activity. Uh and so more competition among sellers will keep prices a little bit more uh affordable and attainable. While in the Northeast and Midwest, uh we expect more growth, >> you know. So, so some improvement next year, but what are what are the factors, Joel, the biggest factors that you think would be holding the housing market back? I mean, would it be, you know, is it rates? Is it prices, the economy? Walk me through it. >> Rates and prices are for sure uh to blame in terms of what's holding the the housing market back this year. But really, I think uncertainty surrounding the economy in general is really holding people on the sidelines. you know, people who are worried about their jobs are not looking to make giant purchases like a new home. And so, I think anything that can be done to uh assuage those fears and build up consumer confidence will really go a long way in boosting the housing market in 2026. >> A question for for those who rent, Joel, and maybe watching this, you say rents expected to decline slightly by 1% next year. You see, you know, a question that that's sort of asked, well, is renting more financially attractive than buying? You'll hear that put out there. That's a discussion point. As an economist, how do you try and think through that? What are the variables? The most important things to think about are the time horizon and whether you consider being able to live in your investment valuable or not. uh based on our price projections, you know, that uh putting money into the S&P and renting might be a good way to get returns on your investment. Uh but getting your foot in the door for home ownership is important for renters, too. It's been especially difficult for first home time home buyers in recent years with mortgage rates remaining high. Uh those who don't have equity from an existing home have to save up what they can from their paychecks to make a down payment on their very first home. And that's been a challenge. So, I certainly don't blame anyone who decides that renting is the right thing for them right now. Um, but this should be another year of rent declines and a good time to save up uh for a down payment in the future if that's what your goals are. >> Felro, just talk to me about the role the Fed plays here. The expectation is, you know, the cuts continue. How does that play into your forecast? >> Right. Our forecast is mostly built on uh economic in inputs um like uh GDP for the various local that we look at uh in terms of employment uh because the labor market always underpins the housing market. Uh but the Federal Reserve is something that we keep an eye on uh how they're behaving and what we expect out of them. Uh like we said a little bit earlier uh on the show that uh the the December rate cut uh is pretty well priced in already. Uh but we're interested to see what uh the chairman and the FOMC has to say about the longer term projections for rate cuts and whether those will give mortgage rates room to even get below our projections. You know, we're we're in the business of selling homes.

videos.title: Housing Market Update: Home Prices, Mortgage Rates & Outlook
videos.text: In today's video, I want to give you a housing market updates and we're focusing on home prices, mortgage interest rates, housing affordability and we're going to focus on first-time home buyers and what to expect. Okay, so we're going to get started by looking at home prices at the national level and then after that, we're going to take a look at the local level. So you're looking at a five-year chart. This is the median sale price of a home in the US and home prices are up by 2.0% year-over-year. Therefore, I mean it's undeniable that there is no housing market crash. During the last housing market crash that started in 2008, home prices fell by 28% at the national level. Again, right now home prices are up by 2%. And as you can see by the five-year charts, there's no crash, you know, which I said to expect for the, you know, every year for the past five years and anyone can confirm that. All right, now let's take a look at the local level. Year-over-year of the 300 largest metros in the US, home prices have fallen in 77 of those 300 markets. And the darker the red, the more severely that home prices have fallen. As you can see, Florida has seen the worst of it followed by California and Texas. So if you take a look at the image, Punta Gorda, Florida has fallen the most down 7.9% year-over-year. But if you look at the rest of Florida, take a look at Miami, it's only down by 2.1%, Orlando is down by 2.7% and Tampa is down by 2.8%. So yeah, those are down. I wouldn't classify that as a, you know, crash. It's just my opinion that housing still remains unaffordable and housing prices have not crashed. And now, I want to show you where home prices have been going up over the past year. Of the 300 largest metros in the US, home prices have went up in 223 of the 300 metros. And as you can see from the map, it's not as concentrated as the metros that are going down. So I don't see a point in, you know, listing out specific local locations. So, I know there's a lot of housing market crash clickbait out there. I understand that people need to clickbait for their livelihood, but personally, you know, I don't, so I can afford to tell you the truth. And this is the situation, and that's why nationally home prices are going up. Because in more places than not, home prices continue to inflate, and housing remains unaffordable. That's the truth. Now, let's address the supply of homes. And this is important because home prices are simply determined by supply and demand. And if you want home prices to crash, then you need a flood of new sellers, but that's not happening. Year-over-year, the supply of homes has increased by less than 1%. The number of homes for sale is up by 0.7%. Okay, so as we're looking at this data the supply of homes, does this indicate that a housing market crash is incoming? Doesn't look like it to me. So, what did I predict in January? That there's not going to be a housing market crash this year, no housing price surge either. What did I say? I I said it's going to be a boring year. I said flat to up 2%, and that's what we're on pace for halfway into the year. So, my opinion that I issued in January still stands. Now, I want to show you the stats, which I believe doesn't get enough attention. So, this is a five-year chart. It shows what percentage of homes are currently being sold above list price. And I think that this is very important because I've been telling everyone over the past five years that you shouldn't be waiting for a housing market crash. Like, if you're ready to purchase a home, then I like I've always been saying, go shopping, find a good deal, which, you know, I know, you know, realistically, I know that's not easy, it takes time. And if you find a good deal, just know that 24.9% of homes are still being sold above list price. And I just want to say that historically speaking that this isn't the normal range. That's about one out of every four homes is being sold above list price. But this is I mean if you look at the chart, this is a big improvement compared to 4 years ago when almost 50% of homes were being sold above list price. And I want to show you this. So I always like revisiting this migration chart. This shows where people are leaving and moving to. So there's still larger outflows from California, New York, Illinois, and Washington. Florida still remains a top states where people continue to migrate towards. It used to be Texas in second place. However, that's now stabilized with now a small net inflow into Texas. Second place is now Arizona. You know, I'm going to be honest with you. Like I wasn't expecting that Arizona to be in second place. If you've moved there or you're moving to Arizona, please explain yourself. Please share why you know, you moved you you selected Arizona. Like I'm curious to know why. Now, let's take a look at mortgage interest rates. This is a 10-year chart of the average interest rate on a 30-year fixed mortgage. Take a look at the good old days when the 30-year fixed was at 3% or below. And that was 2020, 2021, and 2022. In late 2023, mortgage interest rates nearly hit 8%. Since then, rates have been on a downward trend, but that downward trend reversed higher right here when we went to war with Iran. So the reason for the reversal in the trend where interest rates started going higher was because energy prices shot up because of the Strait of Hormuz. So this caused the rate of inflation to go up. And with higher inflation expectations, the interest rates on government debts went up. And if interest rates on government debt goes up, then mortgage interest rates follow. They go up as well. So currently, the average mortgage interest rate on a 30-year fixed is at 6.47%. That's because the war in Iran is de-escalating between the US and Iran. Like I know it's a fragile MOU and a ceasefire, like it's very very fragile, especially with Israel. But the opposite of what I showed you is happening. Oil flow is improving, cost of energy is decreasing, inflation expectations are lowering, and the rates on government bonds are decreasing. So the key takeaway here is that if the war continues or if it escalates, then mortgage interest rates are going to go higher. If there's de-escalation, then mortgage interest rates will come down. In terms of housing affordability, I want to show you this. So look at how bad the situation has gotten for first-time home buyers. There is now a record 242 cities in the US that now have starter homes going for $1 million and above. A starter home is defined as a home in the lowest 1/3 of home prices in a given region. In 2020, it was at 80 cities in the US. Last year, it was at 226 cities. Now it's at 242 cities. Okay, so California has the most cities with million-dollar starter homes with 105 cities where the typical starter home is $1 million or more. In second place, you have New York with 41 cities. This is up from 12 before the pandemic. In third place, you have New Jersey with 26 cities. This is up from one before the pandemic. So it's been a bad situation for millennials. It's a ridiculous situation for Gen Z. I have no idea how Gen Alpha is going to afford a home without some serious help from the family. Listen, the golden rule is that you should not be spending more than 30% of your income on housing. Right now, the median salary of a young professional ages 25 to 34 stands at $59,000 a year. So if you want to buy a million-dollar home with today's interest rates, and let's Let's say that you miraculously find a way to put down 20% on a million-dollar home, you know, which is going to be 200k, then to stay within the 30% rule, you need to make $202,000 a year. Now, let's just say you put down 5%, right? Then you need to be making $242,000 a year, which is way more than of course the $59,000 a year that most young professionals 25 to 34 are making. Listen, I'm just going to be honest with you. The way I'm reading this is that these numbers are just not workable anymore in 242 cities in the US. Like if you ask me why this is happening to me, this is simple. Wages have not kept up with inflation. Listen, the only way that housing is going to become affordable again is if home prices crash or if wages start going up more than inflation or if it's going to be a combination of both. But again, you know, I called it correctly for the past, you know, every year for the past 5 years and I don't expect a housing market crash this year. And I don't expect wages to outpace inflation, you know, as usual. Additionally, the housing market has the support of President Trump. Like if you remember that video clip of President Trump saying that he wants home prices to go up. So, I'll tell you that no, I don't expect the housing market situation to improve anytime soon. Like I know that's not what people want to hear, but that I'm just being honest, that's the truth, which is why I encourage people to purchase a home if they're financially capable and not try to time the markets. Again, look for a good deal, you know, take your time. The average time to find a home and close is about 5 months. But for me personally, like my situation was it took me 11 months. So, that's your housing market update. Please subscribe. Thank you for the support and wish you a very nice day. Take care.

videos.title: The Housing Market is About to COLLAPSE
videos.text: The housing market is crumbling. What was once one of the closest things to an infinite money glitch is now turning into one of the most distorted and broken markets in the global economy. Simply put, this is going to be one of the most significant economic shifts the modern world has ever seen. So, in this video, we'll look at why the buyers are tapped out, why the sellers are losing pricing power, why the Fed may actually work against the market, and why this same property trade is buckling from London all the way to Oakuckland. My name is Nick and this is the Finance Bureau. Unless you've been living under a rock, you'll have noticed that housing prices have been going absolutely insane over the past decade. And if you have been living under a rock, it might be a good idea to get it valued as it's probably worth a lot more than you think. Anyway, as a result of that parabolic run, prices can stay stubbornly high while the market underneath actually weakens. Realtor.com's May data for the US showed national median list prices are down 2.4% 4% year-over-year, the seventh straight annual decline and the steepest drop in records going back to 2017. Of course, this doesn't mean that every seller is panicking right now. In May, existing home sales actually rose, but the market as far as price is concerned is trending down and it still looks fragile today with affordability having the biggest impact. In May, prices fell in 28 of the 100 largest metro areas. But many argue that the real problem is the monthly payment. According to the latest data from Freddy Mack, at the time of making this video, at least, the 30-year fixed mortgage is at around 6.5%. That's down slightly from the 2023 levels, but still miles away from the easy money years we saw either side of co. And that, my friends, takes us to the recent price cuts. Now, a price cut is not a gift from a generous seller to the buyer. Of course, landlords and homeowners aren't exactly known for their uh generosity. If homeowners are lowering their asking price, they're only doing so because the market has forced their hand. In April alone, 35% of US home sellers cut their asking price. That's a clear signal that buyers are pushing back hard enough to force a significant repricing. And this is a clear pivot in the psychology of the whole market. A few years ago, sellers listed high because someone virtually always showed up to buy. Now, 65% of US households can't afford a medianpriced new home, at least according to data from the National Association of Homebuilders. That market has a massive wall of missing demand. And there's no shortage of data elsewhere to back that up. According to realtor.com, asking prices have fallen or held flat for more than 31 straight weeks with outright year-over-year declines for 20 straight weeks. It's also worth noting that the market seen the biggest drops in price were the ones running hottest prior to the shift. Uh states like Texas and Florida were among the most likely to cut prices, which is no surprise considering that this is where inventory and investor heat was the most intense. For years, the approach to making money in real estate was surprisingly simple. Buy a house, wait, refinance, repeat. Bob's your uncle, Fanny May's your aunt. But that concept is breaking down in a big way as the model stalls. And we're going to explore exactly why that's happening. But before we do that, if you want to stay ahead of the curve on market shifts like this and want to make sure you're prepared for whatever the future has in store, then you should sign up for the Finance Bureau newsletter. It's absolutely free and we break down the key moves across markets, macro, politics, and more so you don't miss what matters. All you have to do is click the link in the description or scan this QR code on the right of your screen to get started. Now, for about a decade before the Fed's last rate hiking cycle began in 2022, the housing market's unbelievably hot run was fueled by one main ingredient, cheap debt. The houses were getting more expensive, but the loan terms made it manageable. By January 2021, Freddy Mack said the 30-year fixed mortgage rate hit 2.65%, the lowest in the survey's history. And that rate did not just help buyers, of course. It helped existing owners turn their house into a financial gold mine. 2020 and 2021 refinances locked in historically low financing costs, lowered payments, and in some cases pulled cash out. About 14 million homeowners refinanced during that pandemic era boom. But think about what that actually means. Millions of people got the best loan of their lives. Then the next generation stepped in to face a totally different market reality. The scale was enormous. According to Freddy Mack's data, there was an estimated $2.6 trillion in inflationadjusted refinance originations in 2020, more than double the previous year. That means a vastly lower household cost and far more household breathing room. As a result of this, we then saw a locking effect develop over the following years. By late 2025, more than half of the US homeowners had mortgage rates at or below 4%. Selling meant giving up a cheap loan and replacing it with something much less favorable. So people stayed put and that low rate lockin helped freeze supply. But it also inflated the sense that housing could not weaken. About a third of outstanding mortgage debt was refinanced during the low rate period, giving millions of owners a deal that cannot easily be recreated. For them, moving now is essentially volunteering for a worse monthly bill. It also changed how people thought about their houses. When the value of their home equity rises while refinancing is easy, owners tend to view that equity as backup spending power. But that only works when the credit system keeps giving them another option. But builders don't have that emotional attachment. When demand slows, they can cut prices, offer upgrades, or buy down mortgage rates. And in May 2026, 32% of builders cut prices, and 61% used sales incentives, according to data from the National Association of Homebuilders. And that puts existing sellers in a pretty uncomfortable spot. One May 2026 survey showed the average builder price reduction rose to 6% while those sales incentives had stayed above 60% for the 14th straight month. A normal homeowner cannot compete with that. And it seems that sentiment from the buyer side matches this. New homes can come with builder discounts and mortgage incentives, but existing sellers often need the sale price to fund the next home purchase. And that puts the resale market in a bit of a bind. So builder confidence isn't exactly soaring. The homebuilder index remains in negative territory with high mortgage rates and rising material costs weighing on the industry. Real estate brokerage Redfin said in his 2026 outlook that the year ahead would be a great housing reset with affordability expected to improve slowly. But uh that was before the Iran war. So there might indeed be a housing reset, just not the one that Redfin had in mind. And at the very least, it's a very different story from automatic appreciation and upon only market. So when higher mortgage rates killed the refinance boom, recent buyers lost the easy path of waiting a year and resetting the loan lower. The market got used to relief arriving through cheaper borrowing costs. But the problem is that the Fed is not really supposed to be in the business of protecting the housing markets. Its official job is maximum employment and inflation at 2% over the long run. Home prices being objectively too high or politically problematic is not really in the Fed's wheelhouse. And right now, the market is not just talking about fewer cuts. The Fed held rates steady after its latest meeting, but the message from new Fed chair Kevin Walsh was notably hawkish. Walsh put price stability front and center. And the Fed's latest dot plot, basically each officials forecast for where rates are heading, showed nine officials penciling in a hike this year. 2026 started out with expectations of rate cuts, but that story has changed drastically due to the Iran war. Buyers who were waiting for relief are now facing the reality that the cavalry may not in fact be on their way. And it's inflation that's playing the big part here. May CPI rose 4.2% year-over-year, the fastest pace in 3 years, driven unsurprisingly by energy pressures. And that's well above a comfortable inflation environment, even if you factor in the idea that the Fed is likely willing to have inflation a bit higher than the official 2% target. Fed officials have communicated pretty clearly why they're so worried about this. Minutes from the April meeting said several participants voiced concerns that years of above target inflation could affect wage and price setting behavior. Translation, they can't just wave this away. But uh bringing things back to housing, this is a bad setup for real estate. As we mentioned earlier, 30-year mortgage trackers are running at around 6.5% with persistent inflation and firm labor market keeping pressure on the Fed, and that keeps things stuck at painful level. Now, it's important to point out that mortgage rates are not literally set by the Fed. Of course, they move through Treasury yields, inflation expectations, spreads, and lender pricing. But that's not to say that the Fed has no impact at all. Far from it. Traders watch expected Fed policy because it bleeds through the whole structure. And this leaves housing with bad choices. Expectations are now leaning more and more toward the Fed holding rates or possibly even raising them. While calls for cuts are fading as wardriven inflation hangs over the economy, no cuts means no clean affordability resets. Hikes would make the payment problem even worse. And that repricing hurts all the more because people were told to expect relief. Earlier this year, Fanny May's outlook had mortgage rates falling toward 5.7% by the fourth quarter. Obviously, those expectations are no more. And recent buyers are in the worst spot of all. They don't have the old 3% mortgage and they don't have an obvious refinance window. If prices continue dipping while payments remain high, the strategy of just hold and refinance doesn't work. And the longer inflation stays above target, the harder it becomes to justify easy money. Inflation has remained above the Fed's 2% target since 2021 based on their preferred PCE measure. But the housing market, certainly the one that we've become accustomed to recently, was built for a friendlier rate world. And speaking of the world, America is not special here. This massive shift in property markets is global. UK house prices unexpectedly fell for a third straight month in May with leading mortgage lender Halifax saying elevated borrowing costs and uncertainty continue to burden the market. While prices in the UK are falling month on month, the average price of a house remains around £300,000, which is about $400,000. All while buyers are getting squeezed by mortgage costs. So, a quick glance at house prices might indicate that the housing market looks strong, but transactions are becoming harder underneath. The Royal Institution of Chartered Surveyors showed that agents saw buyer inquiries, sales, and prices fall in May. While transactions were taking an average of 21.5 weeks to complete, there is a clear friction in the market, and the warning signs keep piling on top of each other. One recent Reuters poll said analysts had cut their 2026 house price forecast to 1.8% 8% with London expected to fall 0.3% this year because of borrowing costs. So there may be relatively less worry for the overall market. But in expensive markets like London, a small rate shift can hit demand hard. In mainland Europe, the pressure looks a little bit different as it has shown up in rents and politics, but it's no less serious. The European Commission's housing plan includes a ton of work on short-term rentals in stressed areas because tourism demand is colliding directly with local housing affordability. Rome is one such example. Ahead of the 2025 holy year, landlords shifted more homes into short-term rentals. Long-term rents rose sharply and locals were pushed further out. The property market did exactly what paid owners best. Not exactly surprising, but now the EU is acting on short-term rental rules as part of an affordable housing push and is cracking down on the Airbnb economy. Countries like Spain have been moving harder. Late last year, the Spanish government issued a $75 million fine against Airbnb over unlicensed tourist rentals. Part of a bigger fight over whether cities are for residents or just revenue streams for landlords. Europe's affordability strain has become so extreme that there are even reports of startups selling individual bedrooms in shared flats and developers helping friends buy together. The European Parliament says EU house prices rose 53% between 2015 and 2024 while rents rose about 28% from 2010 to early 2025. Now, this might all sound like prices are staying high and the infinity money glitch is working as intended, but when they're trying to sell the idea that buying a single bedroom is home ownership, there's a pretty clear signal that a market is completely tapped out of buyers. And that's a market that has been pushed to the extreme and it's ready to roll over on the first sign of weakness. But what would that actually look like? Well, look no further than New Zealand. New Zealand is one of the clearest examples of a country that relied on property as a national wealth strategy, and they pushed that to the absolute limit. But after the market peaked in 2022, following an epic 25-year run, things turned ugly fast. Data from totality showed that house prices were about 31% lower in real terms from the peak, putting real prices back to levels not seen since 2016. And that has changed the whole New Zealand economy. The country had relied for decades on inflating housing to engineer recoveries. But that approach has failed this time. If property stops pulling the economy up, everything suffers greatly for having been dependent on it. Almost 20% of Oakland sellers lost money in the first quarter of 2026 and more than 40% of apartment sellers took a loss. That's a long way from the old system that property always bails you out. And needless to say, that's not exactly an infinite money glitch. But if New Zealand is a warning sign for what's to come for the rest of the world, how does this actually play out? Well, there likely isn't enough leverage in the system to see something like 2008 again. The more realistic danger is a long grinding reset that damages buyers, sellers, local economies, and politics simultaneously. For a rough estimate on how far the current system has taken us, the OECD estimates real house prices are still over 40% higher than just a decade ago. And that's why younger people are so angry about this. 60% of OECD respondents aged between 18 to 39 worried about housing affordability, compared with 38% of those aged 55 to 64. Older owners got the asset boom. Younger buyers pay for the system that provided it. But even if prices do continue to drop, it might not be the way young people would hope. In the US, household debt reached $18.8 $8 trillion in the first quarter of 2026 with mortgage balances at over $13 trillion. Housing stress is hitting the whole consumer economy and that has a knock-on effect that can create a negative feedback loop. Foreclosures in the US are already flashing yellow. First quarter foreclosure filings neared 119,000, a six-year high driven by higher ownership costs and household debt pressures. And that's some major distress. The payment pressure in the US goes well beyond the mortgage. Of course, non-mortgage costs are taking a much bigger share of monthly housing bills. And many owners who thought they were safe are in for a rude awakening. Rising insurance premiums in particular have made it harder for some homeowners to keep up with mortgage payments, especially in high-risisk states. When insurance jumps, the bank isn't going to lower your monthly principle. Property taxes are another hit. Insurance and taxes now cost more than the mortgages for some homeowners, meaning the idea of securing a cheap rate is only half the story. Cheap debt helps, but it's not even the main bill in some cases. Depending on the jurisdiction, policy can actually make the problem worse, too. Tax favored housing can lift prices and make buying harder for lower inome households and that only amplifies the issue of inequality in the developed world that are reaching a breaking point. New Zealand's housing slump has cast serious doubt on property as a reliable investment strategy with the effects of its downturn being a major drag on the country's economy. Now, why is that a problem for the rest of the developed world? Well, because most other developed countries also relied heavily on the property market during their economic booms, buyers may get more negotiating power as property markets cool off. But that doesn't mean housing is about to get super cheap. For prices to drop considerably, there would need to be serious economic pain. And in that scenario, by definition, buyers won't have much buying power. So, no matter how you slice it, the easy money real estate era is over. The market model has been pushed to the brink. There's only so much you can squeeze out of buyers. But real estate markets take much longer to adjust when compared to something like the stock market. It may seem unlikely now, but plenty of countries will soon start seeing those housing prices move the way they have in New Zealand. The infinite money glitch was nice for those that got in on it, but it wasn't infinite after all. All right, that's all we have time for today, but what do you think? Has the global property market been pushed to its limit, or is the market just in a little gully right now? Feel free to get highly opinionated in the comment section as always. And if you want to learn about how the stock market is in an equally delicate spot, then you can watch our video on that right over here. That's all from me today. As always, thank you very much for watching and I'll see you in the next one. This is Nick signing off.
